blurb:
Eleven years old and still has a spring in his step. Meeko is a 33-pound Jack Russell-Greyhound mix who ended up at animal control and landed at Mostly Mutts, where the volunteers immediately fell for him. He is currently in a foster home and doing great.

He's described as spunky, playful, and a total lap dog. He will climb right into your lap if you give him the opening, and he's not quiet about letting you know when he wants attention. That last part means a house is a better fit than a condo or apartment, since he's got some opinions and he's willing to share them out loud.

Meeko wants to be your only dog. He didn't love other dogs at the shelter, though he's calm about dogs he sees from a distance on walks. He's a little nervous around younger kids, so a home with teens or adults would be ideal. He's well-mannered, smart, and gentle. If you've been thinking about a calmer senior dog who still has plenty of personality left, Meeko is worth going to meet.

He's at Mostly Mutts in Marietta and currently in a foster home. Reach out through their website to set up a visit.
scoring_notes:
• Spunky 11-year-old lap dog defies senior stereotypes and offers readers an unexpected, feel-good adoption opportunity
• Animal control to volunteer-beloved foster home arc creates an emotionally engaging journey with clear newsletter pull
• Senior age combined with dog and child restrictions narrows the adopter pool, suggesting he has been waiting longer than average for the right fit

blurb:
Bolton came into Mostly Mutts underweight with patchy fur, clearly not having had the easiest run of things. That was then. These days he's healthy, happy, and his foster says he lights up every room he walks into. He's nine years old, about 56 pounds, and a hound-boxer mix with a personality that is equal parts goofy and genuinely sweet.

He loves to sniff everything (he's a hound, so that tracks), play a game of tag with other dogs, and then come home and chill. He's not bouncing off the walls. He's more the type to be happy doing whatever you're doing, whether that's a walk around the neighborhood or an evening on the couch. His foster describes him as playful and silly but also calm and gentle.

He's been friendly with other dogs in his foster home and loves to greet them on walks, so if you have a dog at home who likes to play, bring them along for a meet and greet. Cats are a no-go since he's got a strong chase instinct. Kids around 8 to 12 have been a great fit in his foster home, though toddlers might get bumped over by accident since he's a big guy who gets excited.

Bolton went through Mostly Mutts' prison training program and has learned a lot. He's currently in a foster home and ready to find his people. Head over to the Mostly Mutts shelter in Kennesaw to meet him.
scoring_notes:
• Transformation from underweight and patchy to healthy and thriving is a vivid, emotionally powerful redemption arc
• Prison training program participation adds a compelling and newsworthy detail that sets Bolton apart from typical shelter dogs
• Came in visibly neglected and has been through training and foster stages, strongly implying an extended shelter journey that demands reader action

blurb:
Josie has the most adorable floppy ears and a nubby little tail, and she would very much like you to come meet her. She's a six-year-old hound mix, about 35 pounds, and she's been through a lot already: animal control, the Mostly Mutts prison training program, an adoption that didn't work out because her energy was too much for that family. She's back now, in a foster home, and looking for the right match.

Her energy level is real. Her foster puts it at a 7 or 8 out of 10. She wants to play, go on adventures, and be active with you. The good news is that her cuddle level is equally high, so when the day is done, she's ready to curl up on the couch with you. She's affectionate, playful, and sweet with people. She even started giving kisses by the end of her shelter bath after starting out a little unsure.

She does best as an only dog and is not a good fit with cats. Her foster thinks kids 10 and up could work well, since she loves to play and does some gentle mouthy play, though never in an aggressive way. If you're active, have a yard, and want a dog who is genuinely excited to be with you, Josie is worth going to meet.

She's in a foster home through Mostly Mutts in Marietta. Visit their website to reach out and set up a time.
scoring_notes:
• Failed adoption return after prison training program creates a layered, emotionally urgent story with strong reader advocacy potential
• High energy paired with equally high cuddle factor is a compelling contrast that speaks directly to active adopters seeking a full-package companion
• Animal control intake, prison program, adoption, return, and current foster placement represent the longest documented journey in this group, making her the strongest urgency candidate

shelter_name: Mostly Mutts Animal Rescue & Adoption, Inc.
blurb:
The name is Fatty, he's about two months old, he weighs roughly 1.5 pounds, and he is a tuxedo kitten with a long fluffy coat and absolutely no idea how much trouble he's about to be in the best possible way. He came to Mostly Mutts from animal control with his siblings, all part of a Silent Movie themed litter, and right now he's spending his days exploring every corner of the shelter's Kitty Room, chasing crinkle balls, batting at wand toys, and then absolutely crashing in a kitten pile when the energy runs out.

He's sweet, playful, litter trained, and already microchipped. He'll need his kitten vaccine series finished out as he grows, and he's not yet neutered, which gets handled as he gets older. Nothing complicated, just the normal stuff that comes with adopting a kitten this young.

Fatty gets along well with his siblings, so he'd likely do great with another cat at home. Kids are probably fine as long as they're gentle and know how to handle something very small and very bouncy. A home that's ready to kitten-proof the place and commit to a little chaos for a few months is going to have a really good time with this one.

Fatty is available through Mostly Mutts Animal Rescue in Kennesaw. Email or call to ask about meeting him.
scoring_notes:
• Tuxedo kitten with a playful name and irresistible energy is a near-guaranteed reader favorite and click driver
• Silent Movie litter theme adds a fun, shareable hook that elevates the blurb above standard kitten fare
• Young age and shelter origin from animal control suggest a recent intake, limiting urgency but not newsletter charm

shelter_name: Mostly Mutts Animal Rescue & Adoption, Inc.
blurb:
Iceman was adopted from Mostly Mutts as a kitten, grew up in a home he loved, and then life happened. His family's situation changed, and now this five-year-old guy is back and looking for round two. He's already proven he can be a great house cat. He's litter trained, up to date on vaccines, neutered, microchipped, and combo tested negative. The hard work is already done.

He's a hang-with-you kind of cat. Likes to be near his people, watch what's going on, soak up attention. He's not a lap-every-second cat, but he wants to be in the room with you. His foster is still getting to know him, so more personality details are coming, but his previous family's feedback was straightforward: sweet, good in a home, just not a fan of other male cats.

If you have a female cat at home who might enjoy a calm, easygoing companion, Iceman could be a solid fit. He did great with kids in a previous foster situation, so a family home is on the table. Dogs are an unknown, but a meet and greet can sort that out.

He's located in Kennesaw through Mostly Mutts. Adoption fee is $175. Call or email to get the conversation started.
scoring_notes:
• Fully vetted, neutered, and microchipped second-chance adult cat offers readers a low-effort, high-reward adoption
• Return-through-no-fault backstory is relatable and emotionally resonant without being complicated or off-putting
• Situation described as recently returned with foster still learning his personality, suggesting relatively fresh but notable shelter stay

shelter_name: Mostly Mutts Animal Rescue & Adoption, Inc.
blurb:
Her foster mom calls her the best cat ever, and honestly, that tracks. Gina is an 11-month-old domestic shorthair who came in as part of the Go-Gos litter, got adopted, and then came back through no real fault of her own. She started having some gastrointestinal issues, and her previous family wasn't able to manage it. Months of vet care and food adjustments have helped, though she does still vomit occasionally and the cause hasn't been pinned down. That's the honest truth, and it's worth knowing going in.

What's also worth knowing: she's playful, sweet, independent when she wants to be, and absolutely loves to snuggle when the mood hits. She's tiny, barely five pounds, and will likely stay that way. If you've always wanted a cat that holds onto that kitten energy long past kittenhood, Gina is genuinely that cat.

Because she's on a special diet, a home where she can eat separately from other pets is ideal. She's lived happily with adult cats before and didn't bat an eye at a leashed dog, so the right multi-pet home could work with a little planning. She's also great with gentle kids. Someone out there is the right match for this girl. Her foster clearly adores her.

Gina is available through Mostly Mutts Animal Rescue in Kennesaw. Reach out by phone or email to start the process and set up a meet.
scoring_notes:
• Foster-adored underdog with a return story and honest health disclosure that builds rare reader trust
• GI mystery and special diet narrow the pool but create a heartfelt 'right match' narrative perfect for newsletter appeal
• Months of vet care post-return signal extended shelter involvement, adding urgency for the ideal adopter to step forward
